```{r, 12 precision analysis, eval=run_intensive_checks}
         iteration         = precision_plan$iteration,
         n_blocks_override = precision_plan$n_blocks_grid),
    function(true_r_value, iteration, n_blocks_override) {
      run_single_recovery(true_r_value, iteration,
                          stimulus_info = stimulus_info,
                          n_blocks = n_blocks,
                          n_blocks_override = n_blocks_override)
    },
    .options = furrr_options(seed = TRUE)
  )
  saveRDS(precision_results, precision_file)
  cat("Precision analysis saved.\n")
} else if (file.exists(precision_file)) {
  precision_results <- readRDS(precision_file)
  cat("Precision analysis loaded.\n")
}

if (exists("precision_results")) {
  precision_summary <- precision_results |>
    group_by(true_r_value, n_trials) |>
    summarise(
      median_ci_width = median(ci_width_95, na.rm = TRUE),
      coverage        = mean(covers_truth, na.rm = TRUE),
      mean_bias       = mean(estimated_r_mean - true_r_value, na.rm = TRUE),
      n_datasets      = n(),
      .groups = "drop"
    )

  print(precision_summary)

  p_width <- ggplot(precision_summary,
                    aes(x = n_trials, y = median_ci_width,
                        color = factor(true_r_value),
                        group = factor(true_r_value))) +
    geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
    geom_point(size = 3) +
    scale_color_viridis_d(option = "plasma", name = "True r") +
    scale_x_continuous(breaks = unique(precision_summary$n_trials)) +
    labs(
      title    = "Precision Analysis: Median 95% CI Width vs. N",
      subtitle = "How fast does posterior precision improve with more trials?",
      x = "Number of trials", y = "Median 95% CI width on r"
    )

  p_cover <- ggplot(precision_summary,
                    aes(x = n_trials, y = coverage,
                        color = factor(true_r_value),
                        group = factor(true_r_value))) +
    geom_hline(yintercept = 0.95, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey50") +
    geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
    geom_point(size = 3) +
    scale_color_viridis_d(option = "plasma", name = "True r") +
    scale_x_continuous(breaks = unique(precision_summary$n_trials)) +
    labs(
      title    = "Empirical 95% Coverage",
      subtitle = "Should track the dashed line at 0.95 if the posterior is calibrated",
      x = "Number of trials", y = "Empirical coverage"
    ) +
    coord_cartesian(ylim = c(0, 1))

  print(p_width / p_cover)
}
```

**Reading the precision analysis.** The top panel shows how the median CI width on $r$ shrinks as you add more trials. The Bayesian-power-analysis question — *how many trials do I need to estimate $r$ within a tolerance of 0.5?* — is answered by reading off the appropriate horizontal line. The bottom panel is the calibration check: at each $N$, the 95% credible intervals should cover the truth in approximately 95% of replicates. Coverage that drops well below 0.95 at small $N$ means the posterior is overconfident at that sample size — a known small-sample failure mode that recovery-at-a-single-N cannot reveal.

The trade-off between trial count and identifiability is now visible rather than asserted. For experimental design purposes, this is the picture you want: pick a target precision, find the smallest $N$ that delivers it without breaking calibration, and budget accordingly. This is more directly useful than any frequentist power calculation because it uses *the actual model* to make the projection.

> **Note (12-R10).** The precision analysis above sweeps trial count at fixed parameter values. A fuller version would also sweep over the prior space — combining precision analysis with SBC to give a "calibrated power surface." That is a one-line modification (replace the fixed `r_values_to_test` grid with `rnorm` draws from the prior at each $N$), but it multiplies the runtime by a small constant. We leave it as a production-only extension and recommend it for any actual experimental planning.

---


## Simulation-Based Calibration (SBC)

Parameter recovery at specific true values is a necessary but not sufficient validation. SBC checks that the full posterior is correctly calibrated across the entire prior distribution — that if the true `log_r` were drawn from `Normal(0, 1)`, the posterior would on average contain the true value in the appropriate fraction of its probability mass. We use the `SBC` package for consistency with Chapters 6, 8, and 11.

This is **proper SBC in the Talts–Vehtari sense**: data are drawn from the *same* generative model that is then fitted. (This is in contrast to Chapter 13's "cross-model calibration" check, where data come from a particle filter and are fitted with a different mixture model — that procedure tests robustness, not calibration.) Because the prototype model has only one scalar parameter, the resulting ECDF-difference plot is the cleanest possible SBC check in the entire trio of categorisation chapters.

```{r, 12 sbc prototype, eval=run_intensive_checks}
n_sbc_iterations_proto <- 500   # use >= 1000 for publication-quality SBC

proto_sbc_generator <- SBC_generator_function(
  function(i) {
    # 1a. Draw true parameter from its prior
    log_r_true <- rnorm(1, LOG_R_PRIOR_MEAN, LOG_R_PRIOR_SD)
    r_true     <- exp(log_r_true)

    # 1b. Simulate one dataset with its own randomized schedule
    sbc_sched <- make_subject_schedule(stimulus_info, n_blocks, seed = 20000 + i)
    sim       <- prototype_kalman(
      r_value    = r_true,
      obs        = as.matrix(sbc_sched[, c("height", "position")]),
      cat_one    = sbc_sched$category_feedback,
      initial_mu = list(rep(2.5, 2), rep(2.5, 2))
    )

    list(
      variables = list(log_r = log_r_true),
      generated = list(
        ntrials            = nrow(sbc_sched),
        nfeatures          = 2L,
        cat_one            = as.integer(sbc_sched$category_feedback),
        y                  = as.integer(sim$sim_response),
        obs                = as.matrix(sbc_sched[, c("height", "position")]),
        initial_mu_cat0    = c(2.5, 2.5),
        initial_mu_cat1    = c(2.5, 2.5),
        initial_sigma_diag = 10.0,
        prior_logr_mean    = LOG_R_PRIOR_MEAN,
        prior_logr_sd      = LOG_R_PRIOR_SD
      )
    )
  },
  n = n_sbc_iterations_proto
)

proto_sbc_backend <- SBC_backend_cmdstan_sample(
  mod_prototype_single,
  iter_warmup   = 1000,
  iter_sampling = 1000,
  chains        = 2,
  adapt_delta   = 0.9,
  refresh       = 0
)

sbc_results_proto <- compute_SBC(
  proto_sbc_generator,
  proto_sbc_backend,
  cache_path = "simdata/W12_sbc_proto_cache",
  keep_fits  = FALSE
)

# ECDF-difference plot: flat curves within the simultaneous band indicate
# well-calibrated posteriors. The prototype model has only one scalar
# parameter, making this the cleanest possible SBC check.
plot_ecdf_diff(sbc_results_proto)
plot_rank_hist(sbc_results_proto)
```

**Reading the SBC output.** With one parameter, the ECDF-difference plot is a single curve that should stay inside the grey simultaneous confidence band. Systematic deviations indicate specific failure modes:

* **U-shape** in the rank histogram (high at the edges): the posterior is *overconfident* — credible intervals are too narrow and miss the truth at the tails. Action: widen the prior, or check whether the Kalman filter implementation is leaking information forward.
* **Hump** in the centre: the posterior is *underconfident* — credible intervals are too wide. Action: tighten the prior, or check whether `log_r` is being doubly-penalised somewhere.
* **Monotone drift** (skewed left or right): a directional bias in $\log r$. Action: re-derive the Stan code from scratch and compare against the R simulator on a single agent. The most common cause is a sign error or off-by-one in the Kalman update.

For the production version of this material we recommend $S \geq 1000$ iterations. The $S = 500$ version above is illustrative.

---

## Forward Pointer: Multilevel Modelling and Individual Differences

**This chapter intentionally stops at single-subject inference.** Chapter 11 closes with a multilevel GCM (and revised Chapter 11 also runs multilevel SBC); the prototype model would receive the same treatment, with population-level priors on `log_r`, subject-specific deviations under non-centred parameterisation, and the same multilevel SBC checks for NCP funnel pathologies. We do not duplicate that machinery here for two reasons:

1. **Pedagogical economy.** The Phase 0 → Phase 6 pipeline applied to a one-parameter model is the cleanest possible illustration of the persona's workflow. Adding a multilevel layer would obscure the architectural points (the `transformed parameters` discipline, the LFO-CV implementation, the precision analysis) that are most distinctive about this chapter.

2. **The multilevel Kalman model is a small extension.** The structure would be: one population mean `pop_log_r_mean`, one population scale `pop_log_r_sd`, and per-subject offsets `z_log_r[j] ~ Normal(0, 1)` reconstructed via NCP as `log_r[j] = pop_log_r_mean + z_log_r[j] * pop_log_r_sd`. The transformed-parameters block runs the Kalman filter once per subject, exactly as the GCM does in Chapter 11. The diagnostic battery and SBC machinery would be unchanged. **Chapter 15 takes up the multilevel prototype model in this form.** The reader interested in fitting prototype models to multi-subject experimental data should treat Chapter 15 as the canonical reference and use this chapter as the single-subject building block.

This is a *deliberate* truncation, named in advance, rather than a silent omission. The persona's six-phase battery is fully exercised at the single-subject level here; the multilevel extension is one structural change away and is described in detail in Chapter 15.

---

## Cognitive Insights and Model Comparison

The Kalman filter prototype model offers valuable insights:

* **Incremental, Adaptive Learning**: It formalizes how category knowledge can be built up one example at a time, with learning automatically slowing down as confidence increases.
* **Uncertainty Representation**: It explicitly models the learner's uncertainty about category prototypes — something the basic GCM does not directly represent.
* **Memory Efficiency**: It achieves learning by storing only summary statistics (mean and covariance) per category, aligning with intuitions about cognitive limits.

### Comparing to Exemplar Models (GCM)

* The GCM stores every detail; the prototype model abstracts.
* The GCM is sensitive to specific outliers; the prototype model is less so (outliers get averaged in).
* The GCM requires explicit attention weights ($w$) as free parameters; attention in the Kalman filter can emerge implicitly if uncertainty differs across dimensions (though not explored in detail here).
* The GCM's sensitivity parameter $c$ controls generalization globally; the Kalman filter's $r$ controls how rapidly the prototype absorbs new information, providing a different kind of flexibility.

A note of caution about ELPD comparisons in Chapter 15: now that LFO-CV is implemented for both the GCM (Chapter 11) and the Kalman prototype (this chapter), the comparison between them is on equal predictive footing. The rule mixture model in Chapter 13 is conditionally exchangeable and uses standard PSIS-LOO, which is technically valid for *that* model — but the comparison across architectures is still a comparison of fundamentally different generative processes, and "ELPD is higher" should not be read as "this is the right cognitive theory." See Chapter 13 for the deeper version of this caveat (and the ABC-based alternative comparison framework that resolves it more cleanly).

### Limitations

* **Abstraction Loses Detail**: Information about specific examples and within-category variance beyond the prototype covariance is discarded. Hybrid models attempt to combine both approaches.

* **Single Prototype**: Assumes categories have a single central tendency, struggling with disjunctive categories (e.g., "mammals that fly or swim"). Extensions using multiple prototypes address this.

* **Path-dependence and LFO-CV (now discharged)**: The Kalman filter is path-dependent — trial $t$'s choice probability depends on the entire filter state from trials $1, \ldots, t-1$ — and standard PSIS-LOO is an invalid measure of out-of-sample predictive accuracy. Earlier drafts of this material left this as an unresolved caveat. **This chapter implements LFO-CV via `psis_lfo_kalman()`, validates it against exact one-step-ahead refits, and Chapter 15 inherits the implementation.** The caveat is no longer outstanding.

* **Conflation of observation noise and decisional precision (Phase 0 Q3)**: The single $r$ parameter controls both the Kalman gain (learning speed) and the multivariate normal variance at decision time (decisional precision). These are in principle separable by adding a softmax temperature $\tau$, and the resulting two-parameter model would be identifiable from learning *trajectories* (early-trial behaviour pins down $r$; asymptotic accuracy pins down $\tau$) but not from asymptotic accuracy alone. We chose not to add $\tau$ here to keep the model at a single parameter for pedagogical clarity, but the conflation is real — when fitting human data with this model, posterior estimates of $r$ should be interpreted as an *average* over learning-speed and decisional-precision contributions, not as a pure measurement of perceptual noise.

* **Trial-1 indifference (Phase 0 Q1)**: The model has no category bias and no perceptual fluency prior. Trial 1 is a coin flip by construction. For tasks with strong baseline asymmetries, this is a misspecification.

* **Static categories (Phase 0 Q2)**: There is no process noise, so the model cannot recover from category drift, distribution shift, or post-experiment reversal. Adding a process-noise covariance $Q$ to the prediction step is the principled extension and is left as an exercise.

* **Structural priors (Phase 0 Q4)**: The initial means, initial covariances, and diagonal observation-noise structure are fixed by hand. None of these is inferred from data. A reader who finds the model behaving oddly on a new dataset should re-examine these choices before concluding that the cognitive theory is wrong.

## Conclusion

The Kalman filter prototype model provides a dynamic, computationally plausible account of how categories might be learned by abstracting central tendencies and tracking uncertainty. It stands as a compelling alternative to exemplar-based approaches, highlighting the fundamental trade-off between detailed instance memory and abstract summary representation in categorization.

As a Stan model it has a particularly clean shape: with a single parameter and a fully deterministic likelihood, Pathfinder initialization is reliable, parameter recovery is tractable, the diagnostic battery is nearly trivial to satisfy, and SBC is straightforward. That cleanliness is *why* this chapter is the right place in the trio to introduce LFO-CV — the algorithm is most legible when there is only one parameter to re-weight and the per-trial filter is fast. Chapters 11, 13, and 15 inherit `psis_lfo_kalman()` (and its GCM sibling `psis_lfo_gcm()` from Chapter 11) so that the LFO-CV caveat that earlier drafts of this material repeated three times across three chapters is now discharged in one place.

The next chapter introduces a third perspective — rule-based models — which makes categorization decisions through explicit hypothesis testing rather than continuous similarity to either stored examples or running averages. That chapter also confronts a problem the Kalman model lets us sidestep: when the cognitive theory's likelihood is intractable in Stan, what is the principled response? Chapter 13 takes up that question, and the ABC-based answer it develops is what makes the cross-architecture comparison in Chapter 15 finally interpretable.
